title: Springerle
subtitle: German embossed anise biscuits dried overnight to preserve the moulded image
excerpt: Traditional German moulded biscuits made from a stiff egg-and-sugar dough flavoured with anise and lemon zest. A carved mould or rolling pin is pressed into the dough to imprint a design, then the biscuits are left to dry overnight at room temperature so the imprint sets firm before baking. The baked biscuits are hard and dry with a delicate anise flavour that mellows over weeks in a tin.
sourceNotes: Springerle biscuits are documented in southern German and Swiss cookery literature from at least the sixteenth century. A carved springerle board is mentioned in a household inventory from Württemberg in 1544 (cited in German food history literature). The combination of beaten egg whites, icing sugar, anise seeds, and a baked-dry texture is consistent across German and Austrian baking texts from the nineteenth century, including Marie Schandri, Das Bayerische Kochbuch (1858), which gives a recipe consistent with the modern approach. The overnight drying step to preserve the moulded surface is described in sources from the same period.

content.text: is not optional: the skin that forms preserves the embossed image and produces the characteristic puffed foot during baking. The dough is stiff; a stand mixer makes beating the eggs and sugar much easier. A carved springerle board or embossed rolling pin is required for the traditional designs; a plain rolling pin produces plain biscuits.

Beat the eggs and icing sugar together in a stand mixer on high speed for 10 to 15 minutes until the mixture is very thick, pale, and falls in thick ribbons. Beat in the lemon zest and vanilla. Sift in the flour, baking powder, salt, and crushed anise seeds and mix on low speed until a smooth, stiff dough forms. The dough should hold its shape without being dry; add a teaspoon of water if it crumbles.

Scatter whole anise seeds on a baking paper-lined tray. Roll the dough to about 8mm thick on a lightly dusted surface. Press a floured springerle mould or embossed rolling pin firmly into the dough to imprint the designs. Cut the biscuits apart with a sharp knife. Transfer to the prepared tray, placing each biscuit with the embossed side up on the anise seeds. Leave uncovered at room temperature for at least 8 hours, ideally overnight, until the surface feels dry and firm to the touch.

Heat the oven to 150°C conventional (130°C fan). Bake for 18 to 22 minutes until the bases are set and the biscuits have developed a puffed foot, but the tops remain pale ivory, not golden. Cool on a rack. Store in an airtight tin for at least 1 week before eating; they start quite hard and soften and improve with time.
